title: How collateral cash becomes security cash

chapters.label: How collateral cash becomes security cash
chapters.script:
[DIYA] A pool loses one rupee. The loss does not spread like dye through water. It travels through contractual layers, reaches one claim first, and stops only while enough protection remains beneath the next claim.

[MEERA] Start with one hundred crore rupees of collateral. Senior notes hold seventy crore, mezzanine notes hold twenty, and the residual position holds ten. The balances create a map of who can absorb what, but the waterfall decides how the map is used.

[DIYA] The first four crore of collateral loss reduces the residual position under our assumed terms. Senior investors may see no principal impairment. That outcome does not mean the collateral avoided loss. It means the residual investor bore it.

[MEERA] Add eight crore more. The residual is gone and two crore reaches mezzanine. The senior note still stands behind remaining mezzanine protection. Loss has climbed the capital stack.

[DIYA] Attachment is the point where a tranche begins to lose. Detachment is where it has been fully consumed. Those points translate the stack into loss bands rather than decorative labels.

[MEERA] A tranche covering losses from ten to thirty percent is twenty percentage points thick. Its thickness shows how much collateral loss it can absorb inside its band. It does not show how likely losses are to cross ten percent.

[DIYA] That probability begins in the pool. Borrower default, recovery severity, timing, prepayment, concentration, and correlation determine the loss path. A thick wall in front of a calm river and the same wall in front of a flood do not offer the same protection.

[MEERA] Excess spread may absorb early loss before funded balances write down. But spread arrives over time. A sudden wave can reach funded enhancement before future interest has a chance to help.

[DIYA] A reserve supplies cash already set aside. Overcollateralisation supplies asset balance beyond note balance. Subordination supplies lower-ranking claims. A guarantee supplies another party's promise. Calling all four enhancement hides their different failure modes.

[MEERA] Now principal begins to amortise. Under sequential pay, senior notes receive principal first. Senior balance falls while junior balances remain, so protection beneath senior becomes larger relative to what is outstanding.

[DIYA] Under pro rata pay, senior and junior balances decline together. Junior investors receive principal sooner. Senior protection may build more slowly or remain near its original proportion.

[MEERA] Two healthy years pass under pro rata allocation. Much of the junior balance has returned. Then unemployment rises and defaults accelerate. Protection was released before the stress became visible.

[DIYA] A performance trigger is meant to interrupt that sequence. Delinquency, loss, coverage, reserve, or servicing tests can stop junior principal and switch the transaction to sequential payment.

[MEERA] Trigger timing matters. A threshold based on lagged data may activate after deterioration has already grown. A generous cure can reopen pro rata payment while risk remains. The clause protects only as early as its measurement sees.

[DIYA] Prepayment complicates the story. Fast principal under sequential pay can build senior protection. Fast principal under pro rata pay can release junior capital. The same borrower behaviour reaches credit through the allocation rule.

[MEERA] Slow prepayment extends every note and keeps collateral exposure alive. A senior tranche may avoid principal loss while still suffering duration and liquidity pressure. Seniority is not a synonym for short life.

[DIYA] Interest and principal can diverge. Junior principal may be locked out while junior interest continues. After a deeper trigger, interest may defer as well. A single word like subordinated cannot reveal both cash paths.

[MEERA] Loss timing also matters. Defaults can stop interest before principal loss is finally recognised. Recoveries can arrive years later. A model entering only one cumulative loss percentage misses the interim shortfalls.

[DIYA] During the mortgage crisis, securities with high ratings and senior positions were tested by losses, correlation, model failure, and market illiquidity beyond ordinary assumptions. The Financial Crisis Inquiry Commission documented how complexity and weakened mortgage practices transmitted stress.

[MEERA] The history does not support the lazy conclusion that every senior tranche failed. It supports a more useful one: priority protects only within the collateral stress, enhancement, and legal mechanics that were actually built.

[DIYA] Re-securitisation intensified the problem when tranches from earlier deals became collateral for new securities. Loss had to cross a first waterfall and then a second. Diversification could appear large while underlying exposures shared one housing market.

[MEERA] Correlation pulls distant losses together. Different originators, deal names, and geographic labels can still depend on common property prices, underwriting, funding, or servicers. The stack protects best when loss drivers are understood, not merely counted.

[DIYA] Imagine ten mortgage pools each expected to suffer moderate independent loss. A model may predict very few mezzanine tranches fail together. Make the housing shock national and several attachment points can be crossed at once.

[MEERA] The senior note in the second layer then receives loss from underlying claims that were individually described as protected. Protection nested inside protection has not abolished the common driver.

[DIYA] Return to our original pool. The senior note began with thirty crore beneath it. After sequential amortisation, it may have even more percentage protection. After pro rata release and collateral loss, it may have less.

[MEERA] Current subordination therefore belongs on every surveillance date. Original enhancement is historical. Investors need opening balances, payments, write-downs, reserve movements, releases, triggers, and closing balances.

[DIYA] Rating migration can reflect that changing state. A rating is an opinion under a methodology and information set, not contractual enhancement. The waterfall pays according to documents even if opinions move later.

[MEERA] Price adds another dimension. A risky mezzanine tranche purchased cheaply can offer attractive expected return. A strongly protected senior note purchased at a severe premium can suffer from prepayment and extension even without credit loss.

[DIYA] That is why investment quality cannot be reduced to expected loss. Duration, liquidity, optionality, legal risk, model uncertainty, and price remain.

[MEERA] Now stress recovery severity rather than default frequency. The same number of borrowers fail, but collateral sale proceeds fall. Net loss climbs higher through the stack. Frequency and severity are separate levers.

[DIYA] Stress recovery timing alone. Ultimate loss is unchanged, yet note interest shortfalls last longer, reserves remain drawn, and weighted average life extends. Time can injure a tranche before final loss does.

[MEERA] Stress the servicer. Recovery operations weaken, documents are missing, and sale proceeds arrive lower. An operational failure has entered the credit stack through collateral severity.

[DIYA] Stress the account bank. Cash is temporarily frozen. Liquidity support may bridge the distribution, but the waterfall cannot allocate inaccessible money. Legal priority waits for operational control.

[MEERA] Each stress should explain a route, not merely produce a table. Borrower failure changes collateral. Collateral changes available cash. Cash and losses move through priority. Balances change future protection.

[DIYA] Reserve Bank of India directions and securities frameworks establish relevant transaction and disclosure requirements. They cannot determine where our hypothetical senior note attaches without the specific pool, balances, enhancement, and waterfall.

[MEERA] A strong investment file reconstructs the loss path under ordinary, adverse, and severe conditions. It identifies the period in which each trigger changes, each reserve empties, each tranche attaches, and each note extends.

[DIYA] Move the idea to flood barriers. The outer field absorbs ordinary water, the first wall absorbs the next rise, and the inner wall protects the town. Removing the outer wall during two dry years changes the next storm even though no flood occurred during removal.

[MEERA] Pro rata amortisation can behave like that removal. It may be entirely permitted and economically reasonable while conditions are strong. The switch criteria decide whether protection remains when weather turns.

[DIYA] The deepest misconception is that tranching converts bad assets into good assets. It can create a claim with lower expected loss by concentrating risk elsewhere. Total collateral loss still belongs to the capital stack as a whole.

[MEERA] The second misconception is that every investor should fear the same event equally. Residual investors fear early loss. Senior investors fear the exhaustion of all protection beneath them. Mezzanine investors live between those worlds.

[DIYA] One pool can therefore support several honest but different risk stories. The stories remain honest only when their boundaries are calculated from the same cash and loss path.

[MEERA] Tranching does not erase the falling stone. It records who stands behind each wall, how thick the wall remains, and who is exposed when the last layer gives way.
